data.title: CHPY: Don't Be Fooled By The Positive Performance
data.description:
Overview

I believe that 2026 will be another strong year for technology stocks, driven by the growing demand for AI. There's no better sector that is aligned to participate in this growth than semiconductors. However, the path forward is likely to be volatile and filled with uncertainty. Therefore, an income fund like the YieldMax Semiconductor Portfolio Option Income ETF (CHPY) may be a great way to turn this volatility into cash flow. CHPY is a bit more suited for a long-term position compared to other YieldMax funds, since it actually holds the equities that it is writing options against.

Looking at the performance since inception, we can see that CHPY's share price has increased by about 23.44%. The fund has maintained a steady upward trend, due to the optimism of semiconductor stocks. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 59.88% over the same time frame. The main appeal of CHPY is the massive starting dividend yield of approximately 40.3% at the time of this writing. The distributions are paid out on a weekly basis, which makes it an interesting fund for investors seeking a high rate of supplemental income within their portfolio.

Although the fund's performance has been strong so far, I still believe that we should remain cautious here. CHPY still hasn't been fully time tested through shifting market cycles. A scenario where semiconductors experience a prolonged pullback due to a change in sentiment or investor confidence, has the potential to be disastrous on CHPY's overall sustainability. I don't want investors to be blinded by the strong performance and assume it's purely related to the structure of the fund. CHPY's performance may very well just be related to the fact that semiconductors have trended upwards nonstop since the fund's inception earlier this year.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, CHPY has total net assets of $160.3M that is spread across a diverse range of positions within the semiconductor industry. The fund's primary objective is to generate a high level of current income from its portfolio of holdings, which can range between 15 to 30 positions at any given time. Unlike most other YieldMax funds, CHPY actually holds shares of the common stocks they are writing options against. For instance, many other YieldMax funds implement a synthetic strategy, which means that they do not hold common shares of the equities they write options against. Therefore, CHPY's straightforward approach may result in less volatility and NAV erosion over time.

When looking at the holdings of CHPY, we get confirmation that the fund does hold common shares of different equities related to the semiconductor industry. For instance, Broadcom (AVGO) is the largest holding at 12.12% of assets. This is followed by many other notable names such as Nvidia (NVDA), Micron Technology (MU), Lam Research (LCRX), KLA Corp (KLAC), and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), just to name a few.

Although I am bullish on the outlook of the semiconductor sector, I believe that there will be a lot of volatility on the way up. A common issue for many YieldMax funds is how they tend to perform during periods of volatility. While their underlying OTM (out-of-the-money) option strategies are capable of harnessing this volatility, it also has a history of leading to their share price rapidly declining.

This is typically because the inclusion of an option writing strategy caps the upside growth. So when markets experience a pullback, CHPY may struggle to recover from the pullback, especially since the large distributions are still being paid out, which reduces the fund's net asset value. On a more positive note, the written options typically have an expiration date that is around 30 days out. Therefore, CHPY has a suitable amount of time to participate in upside growth, unlike 0DTE (zero-days-to-expiration) options that are a lot riskier.

Performance Insights & Risks

One thing that investors need to understand, is that a high dividend yield doesn't automatically guarantee a high total return over time. When you're dealing with option ETFs that pay out distributions over 20%, the funds are very likely to see their share price decline over time. The simple truth is that these high yields are unsustainable and market conditions need to be ideal for funds like CHPY to perform well. However, this is by design and funds like CHPY should only be utilized when you're bullish on the underlying sector or holdings that it tracks. I believe the performance can be summed up in three different scenarios:

  1. Semiconductors trend upward = CHPY's performance will remain strong as it currently is. Distributions are likely to remain within a consistent range as well. However, CHPY will underperform the overall semiconductor sector.
  2. Semiconductors are flat/choppy = CHPY may experience very slight share price erosion. The large distributions may cause the share price to decline and growth may be limited. However, the distributions may help CHPY outperform the overall sector.
  3. Semiconductors are declining = CHPY is likely to experience larger price losses than the index. Distributions may help offset these declines, but it depends on how severe of a downturn is happening. CHPY is very unlikely to recover from these declines, even if markets maintain an upward trend in the future.

While CHPY's performance has looked strong, I want to remind investors that the fund has an inception that only dates back to April of 2025. The market has maintained an upward trend since the bottom in April, so CHPY is not time tested. We have not yet experienced what performance will look like during a market pullback. Therefore, I believe that CHPY's performance is only a reflection of the positive market momentum, rather than definitive proof that its strategy is efficient.

Additionally, I want to emphasize the fact that CHPY is best utilized by investors seeking a high rate of income generation. The high yield doesn't mean that CHPY will outperform during market rallies. To demonstrate this, we can refer to CHPY's performance since its inception. When including all dividends paid, the total return sits around 59.88%.

This severely underperforms more traditional ETFs that track the semiconductor sector, such as the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) and the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX). As we can see below, a more traditional approach allows long term investors to fully participate in the expansion of the sector. Therefore, a position in CHPY makes the most sense as an accompanying position for investors that already have a diverse portfolio of semiconductor equities.

Dividend Utility

As of the latest declared weekly distribution of $0.3786 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 40.3% at this time. Since new payouts are declared every week, the listed yield can be significantly different week to week. Additionally, the actual payout amounts will vary over time, despite CHPY demonstrating an ability to provide payout amounts in a tight range over the year. This is because payouts are determined based on factors such as the success of the written options, implied volatility levels of its holdings, or the overall momentum of the semiconductor industry. Therefore, CHPY may not be a great choice for investors seeking a consistent and predictable stream of income generation within their portfolio.

Once again, the fund hasn't been time tested through an unfavorable market environment. So don't be fooled into thinking that the fund has some magical ability to keep payouts within a tight range forever. As soon as the sentiment of the semiconductor industry shifts and a pullback happens, I wholeheartedly anticipate that payouts will also decline.

This is just a natural result of option funds like CHPY, which rely on an upward trend for their strategy to remain efficient. In a scenario where semiconductor stocks fall, CHPY will also see a lower net asset value, and the option premiums may no longer produce income that can meet the expectation of payouts.

On a more positive note, CHPY has the ability to provide tax-efficient payouts. As we can see below, the most recent distribution contained 97.08% return of capital. Unlike net investment income which has unfavorable tax consequences, return of capital distributions aren't classified as income and therefore, aren't taxed as such. A return of capital distribution reduces an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale.

However, it's also important to recognize that the makeup of distributions can change every week. Some weeks the fund may utilize a greater amount of return of capital, while other weeks the distributions may be entirely made up from net investment income. So while the fund has the potential to limit tax consequences, I still believe that a position in CHPY is best utilized within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that CHPY is only a hold for now. If you timed entry properly and rode the share price higher over the last few months, I don't see a reason to sell. However, I don't feel confident in initiating a position in CHPY yet since the fund still isn't time tested. I want to observe how the fund's performance may change during less favorable market conditions going forward. Just because CHPY has done a great job at keeping payouts within a consistent range since its inception, while also delivering positive price growth, I don't think it's wise to chase an entry at the current price levels.

data.title: Zurn Elkay Water Solutions: Great Product, Revenue Model, And A Visible Growth Catalyst
data.description:
Summary

I recommend a buy rating for Zurn Elkay Water Solutions Corporation (ZWS) as it benefits from the shift toward filtered drinking water, has a solid institutional customer base, and has a visible growth catalyst like Michigan’s "Filter First" law.

Business Description

ZWS is a provider of water management products and services. Its offerings range from filtered water stations to behind-the-wall valves and drains, which are used to ensure water safety and hygiene. The sectors ZWS serves include education and healthcare to ensure the buildings stay compliant.

Growth in filtered drinking water

In my opinion, the shift towards filtered drinking water is a key growth driver for ZWS. There are a few drivers for this upgrade. Firstly, more constructions (i.e., new homes and buildings) mean more requirements for clean water. Secondly, the increased public health awareness regarding water contamination makes filtration a necessity rather than a luxury.

What’s happening today is that the US is going through the early stages of a major upgrade, wherein it is transitioning from ~8 million old drinking fountains (current base) to modern filtered water stations. While we don’t know the exact number of the installed base already upgraded, management noted the vast majority as legacy 'bubblers' ripe for conversion, which means there is still a huge amount of outdated equipment that needs to be replaced. For ZWS, this provides solid visibility into the near-term demand, given we are still in the early innings of the upgrade cycle.

This upgrade phase, then, creates a powerful "razor-and-blade" revenue model. Every time ZWS installs a water filter, it is not just selling a one-time hardware sale; ZWS benefits from a long-term demand runway for filter replacements (recurring revenue at high margin). With the majority of the installed base still not upgraded, the current P&L doesn’t accurately reflect the true earnings power. As the installed base expands (those being converted), we should see accelerating demand for filter upgrades/maintenance, which would drive up margins and earnings growth.

In addition, I also think ZWS is smart to implement proprietary filter heads, as this basically locks in users (prevents users from using alternatives) and forces timely replacements (once it expires, the user must upgrade). This makes the revenue stream even more steady.

Stable institutional markets

Aside from the product and revenue model itself, ZWS's customer base makes the overall fundamentals even better. The majority of ZWS revenue comes from the institutional sector (Q3 earnings call), specifically education and healthcare. Unlike residential or commercial sectors, the institutional sector typically funds projects through municipal bonds, endowments, and operating budgets that are practically shielded from short-term interest rate fluctuations. Think about it, even if a recession hits, a hospital cannot delay a necessary retrofit (they need to stay compliant, and it’s a necessity), and for schools, they typically already have capital allocated for hygiene upgrades. This was evident in the Q3 2025 results, where growth remains at a healthy low teens (11%) despite the current US macro environment.

The "Filter First" domino effect

A strong growth catalyst for ZWS is the "Filter First" legislation in Michigan, which mandates the installation of filtered water stations in schools to address the problem of water contamination. I see this as a solid game-changing catalyst because it effectively shifts the upgrade from being a "nice-to-have" choice to a legal requirement (enforceable and must be compliant). Although the amount Michigan set aside is not a large amount ($50 million), the message is clear that this is now a “must-have,” and if other states start to follow, the overall demand driver would become more visible. In fact, this “domino effect” is already in play. Michigan is the first mover, but similar bills are already advancing in states like New York, Pennsylvania, Massachusetts, and Wisconsin. If this trend goes national, the market will expand significantly (you can find this in the ZWS 2024 sustainability report).

As more states pass these laws, they don’t just catalyze a more visible growth; we should see upward revisions to consensus estimates as they start to price in higher growth. Also, because states usually provide the funding, there is little friction for end users to upgrade (since money is not an issue), and hence, sales happen faster, given little risk of not getting paid.

Valuation

I think the baseline earnings expectations for ZWS should be consensus numbers, since they have been largely in line with actual numbers. If we just take the consensus FY28 number of ~$2.04 and just attach the past 3-year average forward PE of 26.5x, this implies a share price target of $54, or 15% upside. But as I said above, there are upsides to this estimate if more states pass the same law as Michigan. The way I size this upside would be based on the historical surprise vs. consensus numbers (~12%). Add 12% more to the FY28 estimates; this suggests an upside of ~27%.

Risk

There are some risks to watch out for. Firstly, ZWS is not completely immune to construction slowdowns. Management did acknowledge a lag effect where weak commercial and office starts today could create a sales gap later. Depending on how fast the upgrades move through the pipes, we could see some quarters of slower growth, which would weigh on the headline numbers. Secondly, the tariff, which management estimated to be ~$50 million, could hit the P&L if the tariff rate gets higher.

Conclusion

My view for ZWS is a buy rating. ZWS has a solid market position, product, revenue model, and customer base. In particular, it has a strong growth catalyst, the "Filter First" legislation, which makes the underlying demand much more structural.

data.title: Noble Corporation: Higher Day Rates Are Coming
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Through consolidation, very few players remain in the offshore drilling industry, and the significant replacement cost of offshore rigs creates a moat for these companies. A large portion of long-term contracts are set to start in late 2026 and through 2027. Noble Corporation plc (NE) is well-positioned to benefit from this with one of the best fleets in the market and a substantially improved balance sheet that protects the downside.

A Brief Overview

The basic business model of offshore drillers is easy to understand. Companies like Noble take drilling service contracts from IOCs (International Oil Companies) or NOCs (National Oil Companies) with a set day rate. As the utilization rate of these companies rises, supply tightens, and dayrates soar (with some lag since the contracts are a year or two long), and vice versa.

There are three types of offshore rigs:

  1. Jackups.

  2. Semisubmersibles.

  3. Drillships.

(Semisubmersibles and drillships are also referred to as floaters).

Each of these three rig types operates differently, but what is necessary to know is that jackups are cheaper and are used in shallower waters, and drillships are more high-end and are used in deeper waters (semisubs being in between, closer to drillships). As a result, day rates for each of these rigs are different. Current day rates for floaters (higher with 7th gens) are around $400k, and jackups are around $150k-$200k depending on the specification of the individual rig. The 7th-gen Dual-BOPs are among the most high-end models (excluding Transocean's two 8th-gen Titan and Atlas ships).

Once day rates enter the 90% range, operators start to lose bargaining power, and day rates start to rise non-linearly. And when the utilization rates come down to 50-60%, the cost of upkeep for idle rigs becomes a liability (especially for floaters).

Background/History

Back in 2010, many offshore drilling companies, including NE, took on significant debt to finance new builds (mainly drillships and semis) and expansion during the 2010-14 cycle. Utilization rates and day rates peaked in 2013-14, at 90% for high-spec drillships and $500k-$600k day rates.

The oil price dropped in late 2014, causing drilling activities and day rates to fall sharply to around 50% ~ 60% in the following years. Note that building a drillship takes 3.5 to 5 years, so payment for many of the new rig contracts that were already placed was delayed, and some orders even got cancelled (for which the companies had to pay penalties). As oil prices crashed once again in 2020, Noble and a bunch of other offshore drillers declared Chapter 11 bankruptcies.

Noble came out of bankruptcy proceedings in April 2021 with a much cleaner balance sheet. Calls for consolidation in the industry were apparent; in the following year, NE merged with Maersk Drilling, becoming one of the biggest offshore drilling companies in the market. Following that, in 2024, NE acquired Diamond Offshore for about $1.5 billion.

Other than the cost synergies and $2B backlog that the Diamond deal brought, this series of M&As following the bankruptcy transitioned NE's fleet into one of the most high-end fleets in the industry with the largest 7th-gen drillships.

Utilization rates started to rise again in 2023-24. By the end of 2024, demand started showing signs of slowdown.

Fleet

Right now, Noble's fleet looks like this (per their latest fleet report on Oct 2025).

Drillships 7th Gen

14

Drillships 6th Gen

3 (one held for sale)

Semisubs 6th Gen

5

Semisubs Other

3

Jackups Ultra harsh environment

5

Jackups Harsh environment

6 (all 6 just got sold Dec 9th)

Noble's fleet appears to be more high-end and high-spec rigs. With the recent sale of all 6 of their harsh environment jackups (five to Borr Drilling Limited (Borr) and one to Ocean Oilfield Drilling), it seems they are focusing more on their ultra-deep and high-spec rigs.

The financials of ordering a new jackup are very different from ordering a newly built drillship. It's quite unlikely for a company to order a new-build drillship in the current market; this, of course, can be different for an NOC, as their financial considerations might be quite different.

According to Westwood Global Energy Group:

According to rig owner estimates, the all-in cost to build a new drillship would be over $1 billion. Assuming 90-95% utilisation, the dayrate needed for a standard 12-15% return on investment (ROI) would be $650-700,000 for the useful life of the rig (~25 years). Build time would be around three years.

So, because of this, in 2023-2024, when day rates hit $500k at some points, companies did not order new builds. It's not just the economics of ordering a new build from scratch; there are also cheaper options available for companies.

  1. Currently, some drillships are cold stacked. An example of this is Valaris Limited's (VAL) three 7Gen drillships (DS-11, DS-13, and DS-14) that have been cold stacked. The cold stack fleet is expected to be back in operation in 2027 as the drilling market strengthens. Another option for companies is all the rigs that were ordered in the last cycle (2010-14) and got cancelled as the oil price crashed. These stranded rigs in the shipyards can be entered into the market as the demand rises.

With that said, the number of new drillships entering the market remains limited; this wasn't the case in 2010-2014. For instance, in July 2014, there were 93 floaters on order, which was equivalent to 28% of the existing floater fleet at the time. (SEC filing, 2014).

Also, back in previous cycles, there would be a new generation of rigs that would enter the market before the last ones had their useful operating life and bring their day rates lower; this situation doesn't exist now, as new builds are rare.

Replacement cost for a company like NE is so high that it naturally creates a capital moat. With NE's debt/EBITDA ratio being at <1.4, it means NE is in a healthy position with the downside being well protected. (Note that this excludes ~$400M's proceeds from the sale of 6 jackups that just happened).

Market Outlook and Valuation

Currently, we are at the mid-cycle soft patch with utilization rates coming down from the highs of 2023-24. Many of the long-term contracts are starting in late 2026-27, with lots of white space in between.

It's actually a more unique time, I think, than I've seen previously, where there is a fair amount of work on the horizon starting in '26 and '27, but there is a definitive gap in between, where it's quieter than we've seen in multiple years."

Robert Eifler, President, CEO & Director - Noble Corp's 2025 Q3 earnings call.

Many companies report about a 95% utilization rate on the horizon, and some, like Transocean Ltd. (RIG), say it might reach as high as 100%. Current day rates that are being signed for late 2026 and 2027 are still at the $400k range. This is normal, as day rates tend to lag.

NE's current contract coverage for 2026 has some white space for their 7th gens. This is not ideal, as idle costs of keeping these 7th gens operational are pretty high, and cold stacking them would be hard, as turning them operational again would take months. In their recent earnings call, management said finding contract work for these 7th gens is their top priority.

As we move into 2027 and demand grows, NE's fleet of 7th gens has significant upside potential. Several of their long-term contracts include day rate repricing options, and some contract day rates have not yet been disclosed.

Noble currently has $1.1B EBITDA TTM with a 7-8% FCF yield and gives out ~7% dividend. Historically, NE has been trading at a 5-6x EBITDA range vs. the current ~4.5 range.

As the demand and utilization rates for offshore drilling and hence NE's EBITDA rise and start to trade at the historical 5-6x, upside can be as much as 200%.

NE's average day rate for their floaters is ~400k TTM. If we were to enter 2027 with 95% utilization rates, and these rates were to show signs of resilience for the whole year, valuations by 2028 look very attractive.

The table below shows the share price for three different scenarios:

  1. 95% utilization rates, $500k day rates, 40% EBITDA margin.

  2. 95% utilization rates, $600k day rates, 45% EBITDA margin.

  3. 95% utilization rates, $700k day rates, 50% EBITDA margin.

Scenario 1 Scenario 2 Scenario 3
Day Rate 500k 600k 700k
Floater Count 24 24 24
Utilization rate 95% 95% 95% x365 days
Potential Revenue $4,161 $4,993 $5,825
Revenue with 95% rev efficiency $3,953 $4,744 $5,534
EBITDA margin 40% 45% 50%
EBITDA $1,581 $2,135 $2,767
X5 EBITDA multiple $50/share $67/share $87/share 158.8M shares
X6 EBITDA multiple $60/share $81/share $105/share

The current EBITDA margin of Noble is around 30-35%, as day rate growth margins start to improve since OPEX costs of rigs remain the same.

Note that this includes only floater revenue; jackups would add a smaller additional contribution.

Oil Prices

Ultimately, the economics of offshore drilling are tied to the oil prices. IEA expects oil demand to increase slowly and plateau by 2030 at 105-106 mb/d. With ample production capacity and record-high oil on water since 2020, near-term oil price growth doesn't seem likely. Obviously, lower oil prices would put a limitation on day rate growth.

However, over the years, breakeven oil prices for offshore drilling have fallen considerably (with lower oil prices, offshore drilling is economical), allowing operators to pay higher day rates at oil prices that would have been uneconomic back in 2014-18.

Recent reports and analyses from multiple sources, like Primary Vision, also show North American shale's tier 1 inventory is getting depleted, which makes offshore drilling a more viable option for oil production.

Risks

  • Additional rigs that might enter the market (whether cold-stacked ones or shipyard ones) can bring the utilization and day rates down.

  • For day rates to grow in a meaningful manner, utilization rates should remain high for a prolonged period of time.

  • Prolonged white space on Noble's contract coverage, especially on their 7th gens, will result in a significant cash burn.

  • Any sudden oil price crash for whatever reason (say a global pandemic) will result in cancellation of the contracts and a subsequent downturn for offshore drillers.

Conclusion

I think the long-term view for Noble Corp is positive, both due to a favourable market outlook toward offshore drilling and Noble's position in the market with a clean balance sheet and advanced fleet. Overall, I recommend a buy on this stock with a 150% to 200% upside price target by late 2027-28.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
